items.title: Best Apps for Focus (2026): Focus Friend, Forest, Focus Traveller
items.summary: Distractions? What distractions? Here are our recommendations for apps that help you stay focused on the task at hand.

items.title: ‘Saros’ Shows Off the PS5’s DualSense Tricks
items.summary: The new game from the creators of Returnal goes all-in on the PlayStation’s haptics and 3D audio. Maybe it will catch on with other game developers.

items.title: Discord Sleuths Gained Unauthorized Access to Anthropic’s Mythos
items.summary: Plus: Spy firms tap into a global telecom weakness to track targets, 500,000 UK health records go up for sale on Alibaba, Apple patches a revealing notification bug, and more.

items.title: Anthropic created a test marketplace for agent-on-agent commerce
items.summary: In a recent experiment, Anthropic created a classified marketplace where AI agents represented both buyers and sellers, striking real deals for real goods and real money.

items.title: Maine’s governor vetoes data center moratorium
items.summary: L.D. 307 would have imposed the country’s first statewide moratorium on new data centers — lasting, in this case, until November 1, 2027.

items.title: OpenAI CEO apologizes to Tumbler Ridge community
items.summary: In a letter to the residents of Tumbler Ridge, Canada, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said he is “deeply sorry” that his company failed to alert law enforcement about the suspect in a recent mass shooting.

items.title: Trump cancels US envoys' trip to Pakistan for talks on Iran war
items.summary: Iran had earlier said there were no plans for a direct meeting with a US delegation led by Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner.

items.title: Explosions and gunfire as armed groups launch co-ordinated attacks across Mali
items.summary: Witnesses report clashes in the centre and north, in what has been described as the largest jihadist attack in years.

items.title: Mexico says US agents killed in crash weren't permitted to operate there
items.summary: The two Americans who reportedly worked for the CIA died in a car crash after a Mexican-led operation to destroy a drug lab.

items.title: This little-known ETF is up over 600% amid U.S.-Iran war, a better trade than oil or energy stocks
items.summary: The Breakwave Tanker Shipping ETF (BWET) has surged more than 600% since the beginning of the year, vastly outperforming crude oil and energy stocks.

items.summary: President Donald Trump, asked on CNBC's "Squawk Box" this week about a possible UAE swap line, appeared to say he is in favor of it.

items.title: Nuclear reactor company X-energy shares surge 27% as AI drives interest in its IPO
items.summary: Advanced nuclear reactor company X-energy began trading Friday as the AI boom and electrification broadly spark a flurry of interest in the nuclear industry.

items.title: Stocks Sink in Broad AI Rout Sparked by China's DeepSeek
items.summary: U.S. stocks were mostly lower, with the Nasdaq leading declines as makers of AI infrastructure suffered steep falls, many in the double digits. Nvidia was down 16%.

items.title: Comex Gold, Silver Settle Lower
items.summary: Gold settled 1.4% lower, down two of the past three sessions, and silver fell 2.5%, down three of the past four sessions.

items.title: DeepSeek Won't Sink U.S. AI Titans
items.summary: Panic fueling the selloff of Nvidia, Broadcom and other tech giants is overblown.

items.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
items.summary: A gut bacterium may be quietly fueling depression through an unexpected chemical twist. Researchers found that when Morganella morganii interacts with a common pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation—something strongly linked to depression. This finding helps explain how gut microbes can influence brain health at a molecular level. It also raises the possibility of new treatments that target the immune system rather than just the brain.

items.title: Gravitational waves may have created dark matter in the early universe
items.summary: In the chaotic first moments after the Big Bang, ripples in spacetime may have done more than just echo through the cosmos—they could have helped create dark matter itself. New research suggests that faint, ancient gravitational waves might have transformed into particles that eventually became the invisible substance shaping galaxies today.

items.title: New “optical tornado” technology could transform quantum communication
items.summary: Scientists have created tiny “optical tornadoes” — swirling beams of light that twist like miniature whirlwinds — using a surprisingly simple setup based on liquid crystals. Instead of relying on complex nanotechnology, the team used self-organizing structures called torons to trap and manipulate light, causing it to spiral and rotate in intricate ways. Even more impressively, they achieved this effect in light’s most stable, lowest-energy state, making it far easier to generate laser-like beams with these unusual properties.

items.title: The earliest evidence of the first stars may lie in a distant gas clump
items.summary: James Webb data reveal pristine gas irradiated by energetic light some 450 million years after the Big Bang — a sign it may house primordial stars.

items.title: Ancient DNA tests the notion that allergies are due to our dirtier past
items.summary: An analysis of ancient DNA and modern disease risk suggests some immune genes may reduce allergy risk rather than increase it.

items.title: The secret to perfect espresso? It’s physics
items.summary: Inspired by gas and liquid flow in earth science, researchers brewed an equation to calculate the speed of water percolation through ground coffee.
